stories.headline: DOJ sues Denver over 37-year-old assault weapons ban after mayor vows to fight back
stories.summary:
The U.S. Department of Justice filed suit against Denver on Tuesday over the city's longstanding ban on assault weapons and large-capacity magazines, one day after Mayor Mike Johnston stood on the steps of the Denver City and County Building and publicly vowed to defend the ordinance. The lawsuit argues Denver's ban violates the Second Amendment's right to keep and bear arms. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said in a statement that "the Constitution is not a suggestion and the Second Amendment is not a second-class right."

Denver's ordinance, in place since 1989, prohibits the sale or possession of assault weapons, defined as semiautomatic pistols or centerfire rifles with detachable magazines capable of carrying more than 15 rounds, as well as semiautomatic shotguns with folding stocks or high-capacity magazines. Mayor Johnston said too many Coloradans have been lost to assault weapon violence and that the city has made significant changes that made the state safer.

The DOJ also separately demanded that Colorado end its state-level ban on large-capacity magazines, though no complaint had been filed against the state as of Tuesday afternoon. Barry Arrington, former chief counsel for Rocky Mountain Gun Owners, is now acting chief of the DOJ's Second Amendment Section and is listed among attorneys on the complaint.

Rocky Mountain Gun Owners president Dudley Brown welcomed the lawsuit, saying Denver politicians had spent decades "pretending the Second Amendment stops at the city limits." Denver City Attorney Miko Brown noted in the city's response that Rocky Mountain Gun Owners previously sued over Colorado's large-capacity magazine ban in 2022 but withdrew the case in 2024 after failing to present sufficient expert testimony.

stories.headline: Avalanche beat Wild 5-2 in Game 2, take 2-0 series lead with MacKinnon's three-point night
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche extended their unbeaten postseason run with a 5-2 victory over the Minnesota Wild in Game 2 of the Western Conference Second Round on Tuesday night at Ball Arena in Denver. Nathan MacKinnon recorded a goal and two assists, while Martin Necas and Gabriel Landeskog each added a goal and an assist. Goaltender Scott Wedgewood made 29 saves.

Colorado has yet to lose a game in the 2026 Stanley Cup Playoffs, having swept their first-round opponent and now taking a 2-0 series lead over Minnesota. Coach Jared Bednar credited improved special teams play as the key difference, noting the Avalanche were sharp in all situations after a strong penalty kill performance.

Kirill Kaprizov and Marcus Johansson scored for the Wild, whose starter Filip Gustavsson allowed four goals in his first start of the postseason. Minnesota coach John Hynes acknowledged that special teams were the deciding factor and said his team needs to regroup before Game 3.

Game 3 is scheduled for Saturday in St. Paul, Minnesota, with a 9 p.m. ET start on HBO Max, truTV and TNT. The Wild captain Jared Spurgeon said the team has an opportunity to take the first game on home ice and noted that no single player is to blame for the team's early-series struggles.

stories.headline: Spring snowstorm brings heavy snow to Denver metro, closes schools and delays hundreds of flights
stories.summary:
A potent spring snowstorm moved into the Denver metro area Tuesday evening, bringing heavy, wet snow that continued into Wednesday morning, prompting school closures, flight delays and a Winter Storm Warning for much of the Front Range. Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools both announced Wednesday closures, and numerous other districts issued delays. More than 500 flights at Denver International Airport were delayed Tuesday as the storm intensified.

The National Weather Service issued a Winter Storm Warning from 8 p.m. Tuesday through 3 p.m. Wednesday for areas including Fort Collins, Boulder, Denver and Castle Rock, with expected snowfall totals of 3 to 8 inches along the urban corridor and up to 12 inches near the foothills and across the Palmer Divide. A high-end scenario projected up to 7 inches in downtown Denver and 13 inches in Boulder. Colorado's snowpack entered the storm at 19 percent of normal for the date, near an all-time record low.

The City and County of Denver activated its cold weather shelter plan Tuesday as overnight temperatures were expected to drop sharply. Downed tree limbs and scattered power outages were reported along the Front Range, and road conditions became slick for the Wednesday morning commute. Red Rocks Amphitheatre canceled its May 6 Bright Eyes concert and rescheduled the show for the following Tuesday.

Snow was expected to taper off by Wednesday afternoon, with skies clearing by evening. However, a Freeze Watch was issued for Wednesday night into Thursday morning along the I-25 corridor, with lows forecast to dip into the low 20s. Temperatures were projected to rebound into the mid-to-upper 60s by Thursday.

stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers abandon pair of bills that would have allowed housing on smaller residential lots
stories.summary:
Colorado Democrats dropped two housing density bills that would have allowed homebuilding on smaller lots statewide, marking a rare setback for the legislature's sustained push to increase housing supply and lower costs. House Bill 1114 would have barred local governments from requiring lot sizes exceeding 2,000 square feet, while House Bill 1308 would have permitted developers to split lots to build two single-family homes where only one was previously allowed.

Both bills passed the House with strong Democratic support but lacked the votes to clear the more moderate Senate. At the sponsors' request, the Senate's Local Government and Housing Committee voted in April to kill both measures rather than send them to the full chamber for a vote.

The failure followed recent local election results that signaled suburban resistance to state-directed density increases. Littleton voters in November enshrined single-family zoning in the city charter, and Lakewood voters in April repealed four zoning ordinances aimed at allowing smaller lots and more duplexes. City officials had argued throughout the session that local governments need flexibility to manage traffic and protect limited water resources.

Housing advocates and homebuilders had backed the measures, citing studies showing that loosening zoning restrictions lowers prices and arguing that rules preventing smaller-lot construction push builders toward higher-priced homes. The bills' failure leaves Colorado's housing density push largely focused on multifamily zoning reforms passed in prior sessions.

stories.headline: Colorado Senate committee kills 'No Kings Act' allowing civil rights suits against federal officials
stories.summary:
A Colorado Senate committee voted 4-3 Monday night to kill Senate Bill 176, dubbed the "No Kings Act," which would have allowed Coloradans to sue federal officials who violated their civil rights. Two Democrats — Sens. Dylan Roberts and Lindsey Daugherty — joined both Republican members of the Senate Judiciary Committee to defeat the measure. Supporters had argued the bill filled a significant gap in state and federal law by providing a legal avenue to challenge federal overreach, including potential immigration enforcement abuses.

The bill was a broader version of Senate Bill 5, which has already passed the Senate and would allow lawsuits only against federal officials involved in immigration enforcement. Proponents of the wider SB-176 argued that its broader scope made it more likely to survive a legal challenge from the Trump administration, while the narrower SB-5 has faced successful court challenges in other states.

Opponents included a coalition of local government groups, district attorneys and law enforcement organizations who argued the bill would expose state and local officials — not federal officials — to a wave of costly lawsuits. Denver District Attorney John Walsh told the committee he believed federal officials would never face a successful suit under the law, and that it would instead be "unleashed on state and local officials."

Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser, a Democrat running for governor who has repeatedly sued the Trump administration, also opposed the bill. Bill sponsors Sen. Mike Weissman of Aurora and Sen. Julie Gonzales said the vote left Coloradans without adequate legal remedies for potential civil rights violations by federal authorities.

stories.headline: Colorado Democrats introduce bill to neutralize road-spending ballot initiative
stories.summary:
Colorado Democrats announced plans Friday to introduce legislation designed to blunt the fiscal impact of Initiative 175, a proposed November ballot measure from the Colorado Contractors Association that would require state transportation revenue to be spent exclusively on roads and bridges. Democrats say the initiative would divert approximately $700 million annually away from other state services, threatening funding for education, health care and social programs.

The proposed Democratic bill would reduce the excise tax on gasoline and special fuel and cut certain transportation fees, including road usage fees and vehicle registration fees. The reductions are structured so that if Initiative 175 passes, the net effect would be revenue-neutral. The bill would only take effect if voters approve Initiative 175, and it would redirect what would otherwise be excess TABOR surplus revenue to fund core state services.

Democratic Rep. Emily Sirota of Denver, chair of the Joint Budget Committee, called the initiative "irresponsible and selfish" and said the legislation was a last resort after multiple conversations with the contractors group failed to produce a compromise. Democrats also said they would not move forward with the bill if initiative backers agree to pull the measure before it qualifies for the ballot.

The Colorado Contractors Association defended Initiative 175 as a modest, targeted measure that dedicates just 2 percent of the state budget to road repair without raising taxes. Grand Junction Mayor Cody Kennedy, an initiative supporter, said the Democratic bill inserted legislative will over the will of voters who broadly support road funding.

stories.headline: Metro Denver housing market stalls at median $605,000 as inventory rises but sales remain sluggish
stories.summary:
The metro Denver housing market showed few signs of breaking out of a multi-year holding pattern in April, with the median residential sale price reaching $605,000, nearly unchanged from $604,000 in April 2025 and $602,000 in April 2024, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors April Market Trends report. The number of active listings surged 17.2 percent from March to 11,539 in April, but that figure is down 3.6 percent year-over-year. Closed sales fell 5.9 percent from a year ago.

Single-family home prices averaged $670,000 at the median, up 4.4 percent from March but only $10,000 higher than a year earlier. Condo and townhome prices dipped slightly, with a median of $385,462, down 0.5 percent from April 2025. The median days-on-market was 14 in April, roughly in line with recent months.

DMAR Market Trends Committee chairwoman Amanda Snitker noted that the predictable spring seasonality that once drove Denver real estate — with prices peaking between April and June — has largely disappeared since the pandemic buying frenzy and subsequent rise in mortgage rates. She advised framing current conditions as "stability" rather than stagnation, though she acknowledged a more negative read is possible.

The S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Home Price Index for Denver recorded a 2.2 percent year-over-year decline in February, the weakest performance among the 20 major metros tracked, compared with a 0.9 percent gain for the 20-city composite. Cotality principal economist Thom Malone said signs of a classic buyer-seller price mismatch are emerging, with sellers holding out on low-rate mortgages while pressure to sell may eventually mount.

stories.headline: World's largest bicycle maker Giant Group USA leases Boulder space for new U.S. headquarters
stories.summary:
Giant Group USA, the American arm of the world's largest bicycle manufacturer, has signed a lease for 44,000 square feet in Boulder to serve as its new U.S. headquarters, relocating from California. The company plans to employ approximately 125 workers at the Boulder location.

The move brings a major global cycling brand's domestic operations to Colorado's Front Range, which has long been a hub for outdoor and cycling industry businesses. Giant manufactures bicycles under the Giant and Liv brands and supplies frames and components to numerous other cycling companies worldwide.

The relocation to Boulder signals continued interest from outdoor industry companies in Colorado's talent base and lifestyle amenities. Boulder already hosts the headquarters of several cycling-adjacent firms and benefits from proximity to a dense community of competitive and recreational cyclists.

stories.headline: Westminster's Vantor wins $70 million geospatial platform contract with National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency
stories.summary:
Vantor, the Westminster-based satellite imagery and geospatial intelligence company formerly known as Maxar Intelligence, has secured a $70 million contract with the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency. The contract covers a geospatial platform that the NGA will use for national security and intelligence purposes.

Vantor rebranded from Maxar Intelligence after the broader Maxar Technologies enterprise was acquired and restructured. The company retains significant operations in the Westminster area and has been a major employer in the Colorado aerospace and defense sector for decades.

The $70 million award underscores Colorado's continued prominence as a center for defense and intelligence contracting, particularly in the space and satellite imagery industries. The Front Range is home to numerous federal agencies and private contractors focused on space, intelligence and national security.

stories.headline: Frontier Airlines reports booking surge from Spirit Airlines' shutdown
stories.summary:
Denver-based Frontier Airlines has recorded a significant uptick in bookings driven by customers displaced by the shutdown of Spirit Airlines, the carrier reported. Frontier said it expects a revenue boost as former Spirit passengers seek alternative ultra-low-cost options.

Spirit Airlines, which had been one of Frontier's primary competitors in the budget travel segment, ceased operations after failing to emerge from bankruptcy proceedings. The collapse left millions of Spirit customers without existing bookings and eliminated a major low-fare competitor at numerous airports where both carriers operated overlapping routes.

Frontier, headquartered in Denver and operating out of Denver International Airport as its primary hub, has positioned itself to absorb a meaningful share of the displaced demand. The airline has been expanding its route network and fleet as it looks to capitalize on reduced low-cost competition in the domestic market.

stories.headline: Broncos sign Mr. Irrelevant linebacker Red Murdock to four-year, $4.5 million rookie contract
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have signed linebacker Red Murdock, the final pick of the 2026 NFL Draft and this year's designated "Mr. Irrelevant," to a four-year contract worth just over $4.5 million, making him the first member of Denver's seven-player draft class to agree to terms. Murdock's selection as the last pick in the draft is a tradition that dates to 1976 and comes with its own celebratory week in Newport Beach, California.

Denver selected seven players in the 2026 draft and has received mixed grades from analysts for the class as a whole. The Broncos entered the offseason with significant depth needs at several positions after their AFC Championship appearance last season.

Murdock joins a linebacker corps that will look different this offseason following the retirement of Justin Simmons, who formally ended his career as a Bronco last week. The Broncos begin their offseason program with quarterback Bo Nix sidelined as the team also continues to advance plans for Burnham Stadium.

stories.headline: Mets-Rockies game at Coors Field postponed Tuesday due to snowstorm, rescheduled for Thursday
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies' home game against the New York Mets on Tuesday night was postponed due to wet weather ahead of the spring snowstorm moving into the Denver metro area, with the makeup game rescheduled for Thursday at 1:10 p.m. The Rockies had already moved up Monday night's game by three hours to avoid weather impacts; Colorado lost that contest 4-2.

Wednesday's originally scheduled 1:10 p.m. game was shifted to a 7:20 p.m. start in anticipation of late-arriving snow. The rescheduling marks three consecutive days that the Rockies have adjusted their home schedule due to weather conditions.

The postponement extended a difficult stretch for Colorado on the field as well. The Rockies have dropped five of their last seven games after allowing only four runs in a three-game sweep of the Mets at Citi Field from April 24-26. Colorado entered the week at 14-21 in what the organization has characterized as a year-one rebuild.

weather.notable: About 32 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I was a lousy accountant. I always felt that if you got close enough, it was fine.

topStory.headline: Seven California Governor Candidates Clash in CNN Debate as Mail Ballots Arrive Statewide
topStory.summary:
Seven leading candidates in California's governor's race met Tuesday night for a two-hour debate hosted by CNN in Monterey Park, the first such forum to air before a national audience. The event came as mail ballots have already begun arriving in households across the state ahead of the June 2 nonpartisan primary, lending urgency to a contest that multiple analysts described as too close to call.

Xavier Becerra, the former U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services under President Biden, emerged as the primary target of attacks from candidates across both parties, reflecting his recent surge in polling following the implosion of former Rep. Eric Swalwell's campaign amid sexual assault and harassment allegations, which Swalwell denies. Becerra, who had been polling in low single digits before Swalwell's exit, has since risen to tie Republican Steve Hilton for first place in the latest California Democratic Party survey. Rivals, including billionaire Tom Steyer and former Los Angeles Mayor Antonio Villaraigosa, attacked Becerra over a scandal in which campaign aides have been accused of diverting campaign funds, as well as allegations that he lost track of millions of migrant children during his tenure as health secretary; Becerra denied wrongdoing on both counts.

Hilton, a former Fox News host and one-time director of strategy to former U.K. Prime Minister David Cameron who has been endorsed by President Donald Trump, argued that California requires new leadership after what he called 16 years of one-party Democratic rule. All five Democratic candidates on the stage directed sharp criticism at Trump, blaming his administration for high gas prices, immigration enforcement actions targeting California, and rollbacks of climate policy. Villaraigosa, who sits at just 2 percent in the latest party poll, drew notice for a notably sharper performance, pressing Hilton on his refusal to acknowledge that Trump lost the 2020 presidential election.

California operates under a jungle primary system in which the top two vote-getters advance to the November general election regardless of party affiliation, a format that had raised Democratic concerns about vote-splitting allowing two Republicans onto the general election ballot. A recent California Democratic Party survey showed the share of undecided voters had fallen from 24 percent in early April to 14 percent, suggesting the field may be consolidating. With ballots already in circulation, candidates have limited time to shift standings before the June 2 primary deadline.
